02 What is a High Output Team - YouTube.mp4
A high op-ed team is defined by three characteristics, and those are: number one, clear purpose; number two, clear context; and number three, clear accountability. Now, when we talk about clear purpose, what we mean is that everyone in the company clearly needs to understand why they are doing the work that they're doing. Words, they need to understand the reason your company exists. They need to be able to articulate the dent that you are making in your little corner of the universe, and they need to be excited about the role that they play in making that dent. We're going to talk more about creating clarity of purpose when we get to the module on building your clarity compass. But for now, just know that without a clear why, it's impossible to build truly high output teams at scale.
But again, we're going to talk more about this in a future module. So if this still feels a little fuzzy, don't sweat it. We're going to get you and your team clarity of purpose. I promise. So for now, let's focus on the other two characteristics of high output teams, clear context and clear accountability. Now, believe it or not, we already addressed the issue of clear context when we mapped our value engines. You see, by visualizing your company's value creation, process, and sharing it with your team, you're helping them get a high-level understanding of how your company works and how you deliver value to the market. It seems like a small thing, but this 30,000-foot view is critical for cross-departmental communication and collaboration, both of which are critical for creating high-output teams.
Okay, so we have a clear context. We have a path to a clear purpose. What we now need is clear accountability. In other words, we need to define who is uniquely responsible for what. Now, keep in mind that your 'who' could be internal team members or external team members. External freelancers, contractors, or agencies. We're not building an organizational chart, nor are we writing job descriptions. We are simply defining who is uniquely responsible for completing which tasks. Even if you're sitting there thinking, 'Whoa, whoa, whoa, you know, I'm a team of one right now.' I still want you to go through this exercise because as you scale, that's probably going to change. And even right now, even if you're the lone formal employee, I bet you have outsourcers. I bet you have contractors. I bet you have help. And so, defining who is uniquely responsible and who is uniquely accountable is essential for building high output teams that can scale. Now that you know what a high output team is, let's start by defining yours.
